12 min read
Just now

--

A working AEO system for solo affiliate marketers — real data, real code, zero fluff.

Some links in this article are affiliate links. If you purchase through them, I may earn a commission at no extra cost to you. I only recommend tools I genuinely believe in. Full disclosure: I use most of these myself.
TL;DR: AI Overviews have pushed click-through rates on triggered queries down to roughly 1%, from 1.76% in mid-2024 (Seer Interactive). DMG Media told UK regulators its affected pages saw an 89% CTR drop. Schema markup helps discovery, but a 2026 Ahrefs study of 1,885 pages found no real citation lift from schema alone. Winning back visibility takes front-loaded answers, real citation tracking, and content built for extraction — not more keywords.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Traffic Isn’t Coming Back. Stop Waiting For It.
Wikipedia — a nonprofit with zero incentive to exaggerate — reported an 8% year-over-year drop in human visitors, and pinned it directly on people getting their answers from AI instead of clicking through. If Wikipedia is bleeding, your affiliate site never stood a chance on raw domain authority alone.
Here’s the part that actually stings. DMG Media, which owns MailOnline and Metro, told the UK’s Competition and Markets Authority that AI Overviews were dragging click-through-rates down by 89% on affected queries. Independent affiliate publishers are getting squeezed even harder — one industry newsletter’s analysis found only 4 of the top 100 ranking domains for product review queries are still independent sites. The rest belong to news conglomerates, Reddit, or big brand publishers.
You didn’t do anything wrong. The game changed underneath you. So let’s fix your side of it.
You Don’t Need a $10K Agency. You Need a System.
Here’s the thing nobody selling a $397 “AI Citation Framework” wants you to hear: none of this is secret. It’s mechanical. AI Overviews and answer engines like Perplexity extract short, specific, well-sourced text — and they extract it from pages that make extraction easy.
That’s genuinely good news for a solopreneur. Winning doesn’t require a content team or an enterprise SEO budget. It requires restructuring what you already publish so machines can lift it cleanly, then measuring whether that restructuring actually worked instead of guessing. I run most of my competitive research and fact-checking through Perplexity Pro ($20/month) specifically because it shows its sources inline — which forces a habit you need anyway: never publish a stat you can’t point to.
This guide covers three things. First, what AI Overviews mechanically reward, backed by studies that actually measured it — including one that contradicts the popular “schema fixes everything” narrative. Second, a real Python script you can run today to check whether your content is getting cited. Third, an agent architecture you can build to automate the whole watch loop, so you’re not manually checking rankings at midnight like it’s 2015.
No fluff. Let’s get into it.
[IMAGE: Split-screen graphic — a traditional blue-links search result fading out on the left, an AI Overview summary box glowing on the right]
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why Are AI Overviews Actually Killing Affiliate Click-Through Rates in 2026?
AI Overviews now answer the query directly, so fewer readers click through. Seer Interactive data shows CTR on AI-Overview-triggered queries fell from 1.76% to 1.01% between mid-2024 and September 2025. DMG Media told UK regulators its affected pages saw an 89% click-through drop. Fewer clicks means fewer affiliate conversions — full stop.
Here’s what most guides skip: it’s not evenly distributed. EMARKETER’s Generative Engine Optimization research found fewer than 10% of sources cited across ChatGPT, Gemini, and Copilot also rank in the top 10 organic Google results for the same query. Translation — ranking well on Google and getting cited by AI are becoming two separate games with two separate scoreboards. You can win one and lose the other. Most affiliate sites are currently optimized for a scoreboard that matters less every month.
Is that depressing? A little. But it also means the sites still treating this as “just write more keyword-stuffed content” are handing you an opening.
What Exactly Does Google’s AI Overview Pull From Your Page?
Google’s AI Overview extracts short, self-contained answer blocks — not your whole article. It favors content that states the answer in the first 40 to 55 words after a heading, written in plain declarative sentences with specific numbers, then backs it up with supporting detail underneath, not before.
Think of it like packing a lunch for a kid who won’t read the label — if the good stuff isn’t visible the second the box opens, it doesn’t get eaten. Every H2 on your page needs to work the same way: heading, then the complete answer in one dense paragraph, then the color and nuance for human readers who stick around.
🧠 Copy-Paste Prompt: Answer Block Rewriter
You are an AEO editor optimizing content for Google AI Overviews and Perplexity.Context:
- I'm pasting a paragraph that currently buries its answer in the middle or end.
- The target heading/question this paragraph answers is: [PASTE H2 HERE]Constraints:
- Rewrite the FIRST answer block to be exactly 40-55 words.
- Open with the direct answer — no throat-clearing, no "well, it depends."
- Include at least 1 specific number, date, or named source if one exists in my original text.
- Do not invent statistics. If no real number exists, write "no verified figure available" instead of guessing.
- Plain declarative sentences only. No hedging language.Output format:
- Just the rewritten 40-55 word block. Nothing else.Now rewrite this paragraph: [PASTE YOUR PARAGRAPH HERE]How to use: Paste into Claude or ChatGPT along with your worst-performing paragraph. Swap the bracketed fields. Expect a tight, citable block back in seconds — then check the word count yourself, because models miscount more than you’d think.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Does Adding Schema Markup Actually Increase Your AI Citation Rate?
It’s complicated. Correlational studies from Analyzify and Frase show schema-marked pages cited 2.3 to 3.2 times more often. But Ahrefs ran a controlled test on 1,885 pages and found no real uplift — AI Overview citations actually dipped 4.6%. Schema helps machines parse content; it doesn’t replace genuine authority.
Here’s what most people miss: the pages in the correlational studies were already good pages. High-authority sites tend to be the ones with the engineering budget to add schema in the first place, so the “schema causes citations” story confuses cause with correlation. Ahrefs’ matched difference-in-differences test controlled for that, and the schema-only effect basically evaporated for Google AI Overviews specifically (though it held up slightly better for ChatGPT and AI Mode).
My honest opinion, and I could be wrong here: implement the Schema Quad anyway — Article, FAQPage, HowTo, ProductReview — because it costs you an afternoon and it's the "minimum viable" signal researchers agree matters for indexing and entity clarity. Just don't expect it to save mediocre content. It won't.
“Schema deployed as part of a complete content and entity strategy produces measurable gains. Schema added to weak content on a weak domain produces weak results.” — the honest 2026 consensus, not marketing copy.
How Do You Actually Track If AI Is Citing Your Content?
Standard Google Search Console doesn’t isolate AI Overview performance. You need to query the AI Overview surface directly. Tools built on SearchAPI.io’s google_ai_overview engine let you send a keyword, get back the exact text blocks and reference links Google generated, and check programmatically whether your domain made the cut.
This is the blind spot that burns solopreneurs. You watch impressions fall in Search Console and have zero idea whether Google stopped ranking you or just started answering the query itself without sending anyone your way. Those are very different problems requiring very different fixes.
💻 Working Code: AI Overview Citation Watchdog
# pip install requests
import os
import requestsAPI_KEY = os.getenv("SEARCHAPI_KEY") # get one at searchapi.io
YOUR_DOMAIN = "travel4fun4u.com" # swap for your own domain
def check_ai_overview_citation(keyword: str) -> dict:
"""Query Google's AI Overview for a keyword and check if your domain is cited."""
url = "https://www.searchapi.io/api/v1/search"
params = {
"engine": "google_ai_overview",
"q": keyword,
"api_key": API_KEY,
}
response = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=30)
data = response.json() text_blocks = data.get("text_blocks", [])
references = data.get("reference_links", [])
cited = any(YOUR_DOMAIN in ref.get("link", "") for ref in references) return {
"keyword": keyword,
"ai_overview_present": bool(text_blocks),
"cited": cited,
"total_references": len(references),
}
if __name__ == "__main__":
keywords = [
"how to rank in google ai overviews",
"best affiliate marketing tools 2026",
"how to track ai overview citations",
]
for kw in keywords:
result = check_ai_overview_citation(kw)
status = "CITED" if result["cited"] else "not cited"
print(f"[{status}] '{kw}' — {result['total_references']} references")Run it: python aeo_watchdog.py. Expected output: one line per keyword, flagging whether an AI Overview fired and whether your domain showed up in reference_links. Response field names can shift as SearchAPI.io updates its schema, so print data.keys() once to confirm before you trust the output. I run mine on a scheduled Replit deployment so it fires every Monday without me touching it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Should You Bother Building an Actual AI Citation Tracking Agent?
Yes, if you publish regularly. A scheduled script querying SearchAPI.io can check whether your target keywords trigger an AI Overview and whether your domain appears in the references, on a recurring basis, for the cost of API calls. It turns guessing into a weekly report you can act on.
🏗️ Agent Architecture: AEO Watchdog
flowchart LR
A[Trigger: weekly schedule] --> B[Planner: keyword list]
B --> C[Tool: SearchAPI.io AI Overview query]
B --> D[Tool: Firecrawl — scrape own page for schema check]
C --> E[Synthesizer: compare against last week]
D --> E
E --> F[Output: report to email/Slack]Components:

• Planner — pulls your target keyword list from a sheet and loops through it.

• SearchAPI.io tool — the citation check from the code block above.

• Firecrawl tool — re-scrapes your own published pages into clean Markdown so you can confirm your schema and answer blocks actually rendered the way you intended, not just what you think you shipped.

• Synthesizer — flags new citations, lost citations, and pages with zero AI Overview presence at all.

• Stack: I wire the trigger and notification layer in Make.com so the whole loop runs without a server. For anything requiring more autonomous judgment — like deciding which underperforming page to rewrite first — I hand that decision to Manus, since it can browse the report, cross-reference your traffic data, and draft the rewrite brief on its own.
Is Turning Your Article Into a YouTube Video Worth the Effort?
Yes — YouTube is the single most-cited domain inside Google AI Overviews, holding a 29.5% citation share according to BrightEdge, ahead of Mayo Clinic at 12.5%. Instructional, how-to content sees the strongest gains. If your article is a tutorial, a short companion video roughly doubles your citation surface.
I’ll be honest: I used to think video was a distraction from writing. I was wrong, and the data above is exactly why I changed my mind. You don’t need production value. You need the same 40–55 word answer blocks read aloud over a screen recording, uploaded with a transcript, and
